𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THE ANCIENT COVENANT RECORD

Volume Forty -- Part Two

The Thanksgiving Hymns

HODAYOT -- 1QH

The Hymns of the Teacher of Righteousness and the Community -- DSS Attested

Columns 5 through 8

The Wicked Have Conspired Against My Soul -- But the Covenant of Your Truth Shall Not Be Broken -- I Was Brought Down to the Gates of Death -- But You Raised Me to the Everlasting Heights -- No Man Is Just Before You -- Through Your Spirit I Know All Things

PRIMARY MANUSCRIPT AUTHORITIES

Dead Sea Scrolls -- Cave 1 Qumran -- 1QH-a -- Hebrew Primary

Continuation of the Teacher of Righteousness Hymns

YHWH and The Creator only

COMPARATIVE NOTES AT END OF EVERY CHAPTER

DSS · ORIT GE'EZ · MASORETIC VARIANT · CRITICAL NOTE

YHWH / 𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄 and The Creator only — no substitute titles

All personal names in original Hebrew form throughout

HODAYOT -- THE THANKSGIVING HYMNS -- COLUMNS 5 THROUGH 8

HODAYOT — COLUMN 5 The Wicked Seek to Destroy the Community — They Have Sharpened Their Tongue Like a Sword — But You Have Strengthened Me Against Them — The Covenant of Your Truth Is Not Broken — You Are the Rock of My Steps

1 I thank you O Creator, for you have not abandoned me in my sojourning among a people of sin.

2 For they have sharpened their tongue like a sword and they have spoken against me in bitterness.

3 They have called me a desolation and they have poured contempt upon me and they have tried to overturn the covenant of your servant.

4 But you O Creator have placed understanding in my heart that I should know your truth and not be afraid.

5 For you are my strength and my rock and my refuge — in you I trust and you are the source of my strength.

6 The wicked have conspired against my life to exchange your covenant for lies — but the covenant of your truth shall not be broken.

7 For you are a Creator of truth and all who love your name rejoice in you and are not ashamed.

8 I know that you are with me in the council of truth and in the holy assembly.

9 And I will not be silent nor will I retreat before those who oppose your covenant.

10 For I know that through your righteousness you have made my path sure and in your great goodness you have established my steps.

11 And you have not let the foot of the enemy prevail over me though I walk in the path of danger.

12 Blessed are you O Creator who has placed in my mouth the word of truth and in my heart the understanding of your covenant forever.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QH Column 5 continues the Teacher of Righteousness hymns with a sustained meditation on persecution and covenant faithfulness. The description of those who have called me a desolation and poured contempt upon me while trying to overturn the covenant of your servant provides the autobiographical background for the Teacher's separation from the Jerusalem establishment. His refusal to be silent or retreat — verse 9 — is the voice of the Teacher who, according to the Damascus Document, guided the covenant community despite opposition from the Wicked Priest and the Seekers-After-Smooth-Things.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves Tehillim 31 — in you O YHWH I take refuge, let me never be put to shame — which stands behind Column 5's expression of covenant confidence in the face of persecution. The Hodayot's personalisation of the Tehillim trust formula — you are my strength and my rock and my refuge — places the Teacher's persecution within the same covenant framework as Dawid's persecution by Sha'ul. The covenant pattern of the righteous sufferer finding refuge in Creator while enemies conspire is the foundational covenant paradigm for the Teacher's experience.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Tehillim 31 and 56 provide the closest parallels to Column 5. Tehillim 56:5 — in Creator, whose word I praise, in Creator I trust, I shall not be afraid — is the covenant confidence formula that the Teacher adapts in verse 4-5. The covenant tradition of trusting in Creator against the conspiracy of the wicked is the thread that connects the Tehillim psalms of Dawid to the Hodayot psalms of the Teacher of Righteousness.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 3 — they have tried to overturn the covenant of your servant. The covenant of your servant — brit avdekha — is the Teacher's own covenant commission received from Creator. The opponents' attempt to overturn it is understood not as a personal attack but as a cosmic assault on Creator's covenant purpose. This theological elevation of the Teacher's covenant role — he is Creator's servant in the full biblical sense of the term — is what gives the Teacher's persecution its cosmic significance and is why the Hodayot responds to it with Creator's own faithful response rather than merely human resistance.

HODAYOT — COLUMN 6 I Was Despised — A Man of Strife and Contention — But You O Creator Have Helped Me — You Have Revealed Your Truth to the Humble — The Gates of Death Have Opened Before Me — But You Have Brought Me to the Light

1 I thank you O Creator, for you have redeemed my soul from the pit and from the Sheol of Abaddon you have raised me up to an everlasting height.

2 I walk on a boundless plain and I know that there is hope for him whom you have formed from the dust for an everlasting assembly.

3 And the depraved spirit you have purified from great transgression, that it might stand in the array with the host of the holy ones.

4 For you have caused me to enter into the counsel of your truth and established me before you with the host of the holy ones.

5 I was despised — a man of strife and contention — a spirit of jealousy in the hearts of the men of counsel.

6 But you O Creator have turned aside the faces of all who hate me and you have made me an object of reproof to the treacherous.

7 The gates of death have opened before me and the bars of Sheol have been unloosed before me — I went down to the roots of the mountains.

8 And the earth with its bars closed upon me forever and I said: there is no hope for my cause.

9 But you O Creator have heard my voice and your compassion has not departed from me.

10 You have rescued my soul from the pit and from the depths of Sheol you have brought me up.

11 And I have known that you will not forsake those who seek you and that the hope of your covenant shall not fail.

12 Blessed are you O Creator who raises the poor from the dust and lifts the needy from the mire to give them a place of honor forever.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QH Column 6 contains one of the most intense personal testimonies in the entire Hodayot collection — the descent to the gates of death and the roots of the mountains, and the rescue by Creator. The experience described — I went down to the roots of the mountains and the earth with its bars closed upon me — may be metaphorical for the extreme persecution the Teacher endured, or may reflect a literal near-death experience. The rescue from the pit — I thank you O Creator, for you have redeemed my soul from the pit — is the autobiographical foundation of the Teacher's covenant authority.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves Tehillim 88 — the darkest of all the canonical psalms — which stands behind Column 6's descent to Sheol imagery. The Hodayot's rescue from the gates of death is the testimony of covenant faithfulness confirmed by deliverance: Creator who promised to preserve his covenant servant has done so even in the extremity of what appeared to be final destruction. The descent to the roots of the mountains and the return to the everlasting height is the covenant pattern of death and resurrection that runs through the entire covenant prophetic tradition.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Yonah 2 — from the belly of Sheol I cried, and you heard my voice — provides a structural parallel to Column 6's rescue narrative. Yonah descends to the roots of the mountains, to the land whose bars closed upon him forever — and is rescued. The Hodayot's Teacher experiences the same pattern. The Qumran community preserved both texts as part of the same covenant testimony: those whom Creator has commissioned may experience the extreme of death before their rescue, but Creator does not abandon his covenant servants.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 4 — you have caused me to enter into the counsel of your truth and established me before you with the host of the holy ones. The Teacher's entry into the council of truth — sod emet — is his participation in the heavenly council of the holy ones. This claim — that the Teacher stands before Creator with the angelic host — is the basis of his revelatory authority. He is not merely a human interpreter of the Torah; he is one who has access to the divine council, as the prophets of the covenant canon accessed it. The Hodayot is the Teacher's first-person testimony of this access.

HODAYOT — COLUMN 7 The Covenant Community Is a Planting of Righteousness — You Have Planted a Planting of Truth — A Garden Watered by the River — Its Root Takes Hold in the Rock — It Shall Not Be Uprooted Forever

1 I thank you O Creator, for you have placed my soul in the bundle of the living and have fenced me about against all the snares of the pit.

2 And violent men have sought after my soul because I hold fast to your covenant.

3 But they — the council of lies and the congregation of Belial — do not know that my standing is with you.

4 And that through the goodness of your mercy my soul is delivered.

5 For you have set me as a holy vessel and brought me near to your truth.

6 And I know that through your righteousness you will have all your chosen ones stand before you forever.

7 You have planted a planting of truth in me — its roots go deep and spread to the living waters and its trunk rises to the eternal heights.

8 And its boughs spread to reach all the trees of the water and its roots are among all the waters that flow.

9 It shall become a great tree and its branches shall give shade to all the beasts of the field.

10 And its top shall cover the birds of the sky and all the birds of the wing shall nest in its branches.

11 And all the trees of the water shall envy it for it is planted in the source of truth.

12 And the planting of truth shall stand forever and shall not be uprooted forever, for you have established it in truth.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QH Column 7 returns to the great tree imagery of Column 2 and develops it with greater intensity. The planting of truth in the psalmist's soul — its roots going to the living waters and its branches covering the birds of the air — is the covenant community's eschatological destiny described in personal devotional terms. The tree that cannot be uprooted because it is planted in the source of truth is the covenant community whose permanence is guaranteed by Creator's own truth. The description parallels the Community Rule Column 8's eternal planting — neta olam.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves Yehezkel 17 — the allegory of the great cedar that is planted by the great waters — and Daniyel 4's great tree whose top reaches heaven. Both stand behind Column 7's planting imagery. The Hodayot draws on the covenant prophetic tradition of the tree as the symbol of covenant community and applies it personally: the Teacher is the one in whom the planting of truth has been established, from which the covenant community grows as branches reaching to the birds of the sky.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Tehillim 1 — like a tree planted by rivers of water — is the foundational covenant text for Column 7's tree imagery. The Hodayot develops the Tehillim 1 tree into an extended covenant meditation: the tree planted by the living waters is not merely the individual righteous man of Tehillim 1 but the covenant community itself, whose roots in the source of truth guarantee its permanence against all opposition. The tree that cannot be uprooted is the covenant community that cannot be destroyed.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 11 — all the trees of the water shall envy it for it is planted in the source of truth. The other trees of the water — other covenant communities or covenant teachers — envy the planting of truth because they are not planted in the same source. This competitive imagery reflects the Qumran community's conviction that their covenant interpretation is uniquely grounded in the source of truth — the divine revelation given to the Teacher — while other groups draw on secondary or corrupted sources. The planting in the source of truth rather than in the streams from the source is the theological basis of the community's covenant authority.

HODAYOT — COLUMN 8 I Thank You O Creator For the Wonders of Your Grace — You Have Counseled Me in Truth — No Man Is Righteous Before You — Through Your Spirit I Know All These Things — The Spirit You Have Given Me

1 I thank you O Creator, for you have illumined my face through your covenant and through your great compassion.

2 You have counseled me in truth and I have come to know your wondrous works from the beginning of time.

3 And you have given me understanding of the mysteries of your truth — of your wonderful counsel and of your unsearchable ways.

4 Who is justified before you when he comes into judgment? And who can be found innocent in your presence?

5 For no man is just before you and none is righteous in your sight — not the highest of the holy ones.

6 What then is man of dust and clay that he should understand your wondrous works and your abundant compassion?

7 For I know that the righteousness of man is not from himself and the perfection of the way is not by human effort.

8 But through the spirit which you have given me I have come to know all these things.

9 And through your holy spirit you have opened to me the fountain of your truth.

10 And through your counsel and by your holy spirit I know that the path of man is not established by himself.

11 For without you nothing can be done and without your will nothing is accomplished.

12 Therefore I know that whatever you have done is right, and whatever you have revealed is true, and whatever is in your plan is perfect forever.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QH Column 8 contains the most concentrated theological statement in the Hodayot on the relationship between human nature and divine grace. The questions of verses 4-6 — who is justified before you, no man is just in your sight, not the highest of the holy ones — establish absolute human unworthiness as the baseline from which all covenant theology must begin. This is not despair but the theological foundation of genuine grace: if no man is just before Creator, then all righteousness is entirely a divine gift. The Community Rule Column 11 states the same position as its final hymn.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves Iyov 4:17 — can a mortal be righteous before Creator? Can a man be pure before his Maker? — which stands directly behind verses 4-5 of Column 8. The Hodayot and the book of Iyov share the same foundational anthropological conviction: human righteousness before Creator is impossible by human effort alone. The covenant response is not despair but the recognition that Creator's righteousness — given through his spirit — is the only possible foundation for human standing before him.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Tehillim 143:2 — do not enter into judgment with your servant, for no one living is righteous before you — provides the covenant prayer parallel to Column 8's theological statement. The Hodayot places the Tehillim 143 confession at the center of its covenant self-understanding: the covenant community is not a group of the righteous standing before Creator on the basis of their own achievement. They are a community of those who have received Creator's spirit and walk in his righteousness as a gift.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 8 — through the spirit which you have given me I have come to know all these things. The holy spirit given by Creator to the covenant community is the epistemological foundation of their covenant knowledge. They do not arrive at their understanding of the Torah through human reasoning or scholarly tradition — they receive it through the spirit Creator has poured upon them. This claim — that the community's covenant interpretation is spirit-given rather than humanly derived — is the basis of their confidence in their positions and their rejection of alternative interpretations.

HODAYOT -- THE THANKSGIVING HYMNS -- COMPLETE

Volume Forty -- The Ancient Covenant Record

𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THROUGH THE SPIRIT YOU HAVE GIVEN ME

I HAVE COME TO KNOW ALL THESE THINGS

Hodayot Column 8 -- Dead Sea Scrolls 1QH-a
